Its no mistake that this film is nearly 100% fresh. This iconic monolith of a film has everything that all great works should have. Unparalleled storytelling, unbelievable casting, stunning performances, perfect pacing, beautiful cinematography, and textbook direction and production. So many people built careers based on the work they did on The Godfather, and it's no wonder why. This is the kind of film you can watch once a week for the rest of your life and always find something new to appreciate, new angles to fawn over, and new performances to stun you. A true viewers pleasure, it plays the whole gamut of emotions. Love, hate, fear, joy, despair, excitement, grief, sadness, renewel...the list goes on and on. The gangster movie to start and end all gangster movies. No others exist without Puzo and Coppola’s iconic vision. I'd give it ten stars if I could. I only wish the sequels had lived up to this one. But I'll complain about those on their profiles. Watch this one by yourself with a stiff scotch and a fat cigar.

There is a reason this movie is widely considered one of the greatest films ever made. The Godfather is a timeless classic that gives a little bit of everything in epic fashion. The acting is superb, the romance is bold, the thrill is a true ride and vengeance has it's way. I highly recommend everyone watch this classy classic and study it from start to finish because once you do you'll know what true quality cinema is.

This is one of the great films of all-time. Francis Ford Coppola's masterpiece of storytelling weaves a complex tale of an Italian family's rise in early 20th century America. As the characters develope in the world of the criminal underground, Marlon Brando portrays the leader of the family, rising to become the Godfather, a term of reverence and ultimate respect. This film has few peers in the history of movie making, becoming one of the most important films of all-time.

The Godfather is one of those movies where the reputation somehow undersells how good it actually is. Everything just clicks here. The performances are untouchable—Marlon Brando’s Don Vito is iconic for a reason, but Al Pacino’s slow-burn transformation is the real knockout, quietly turning Michael from reluctant outsider into something chilling and inevitable. The direction is patient and confident, letting scenes breathe instead of rushing to the next plot point, and the writing is so sharp that even casual conversations feel loaded with power and consequence. Add in the moody cinematography and that instantly recognizable score, and the whole thing feels rich, deliberate, and endlessly rewatchable. If there’s any “criticism” to be had, it’s more about taste than quality. The movie is long, and it moves at its own pace—if you’re not in the mood for a methodical, dialogue-heavy crime saga, it might feel slow in spots. Some supporting characters don’t get as much development as they deserve, but that’s mostly because the central story is so strong it overshadows everything else. Overall, The Godfather isn’t just a great gangster movie—it’s a masterclass in storytelling, atmosphere, and character evolution that earns every bit of its legendary status. Rating: 10/10
"A work of art in cinema… an absolute masterpiece."

Watching The Godfather for the first time feels less like discovering a movie and more like sitting an exam you’ve been prepped for your whole life Everyone, everywhere, has already told you it’s "the greatest film ever made," pop culture has spoiled half the moments, and the parodies make you feel like you’ve somehow already seen it. So yeah, going in with an open mind is basically a myth. And here’s the hot take that apparently puts you in witness protection The Godfather is… good. Just good. Not life-changing. Not cinema’s holy grail. Just a solid, competently made movie that you’re glad you finally watched so people stop looking at you like you’ve committed a crime against film history. To be fair, the mob world feels authentic, and the post-war atmosphere is done well The opening wedding scene makes its point loud and clear. Don Corleone never stops working, even at his daughter’s wedding. Message received. Loudly. Repeatedly Maybe just a few minutes longer than strictly necessary

I always had the impression that I wouldn't understand it, or that it would be boring. I kept this impression for the longest time, until I finally decided to sit by butt down, and watch it from start to finish. It was kind of what I expected. Is it undeniably a classic? Yes. Does it have a great cast? Yes. Is it loved by many? Yes. No, I haven't read the book, and I'm still on the fence about watching the other two...
